title: Introduction
content_preview: The doctrine of separation of powers was articulated by Montesquieu in 'The Spirit of Laws' (1748), arguing that concentrating legislative, executive, and judicial powers leads to tyranny and despotis

title: Political Context of Montesquieu
content_preview: Montesquieu's early life coincided with significant governmental changes: England's constitutional monarchy declined after the Glorious Revolution (1688-89) and formed Great Britain with Scotland in 1

title: Montesquieu's Theory of Separation of Power
exact_ocr_heading: MONTESQUIEU'S THEORY OF SEPARATION OF POWER
content_preview: Montesquieu classified government powers into three categories: legislative, executive, and judiciary.

title: Legislative Powers
exact_ocr_heading: LEGISLATIVE POWERS: fork. stal Montesquieu emphasized
content_preview: Montesquieu stressed a separate legislative branch for law-making, stating it cannot transfer this power to others. This prevents arbitrary rule and ensures laws reflect the people's will and needs.

title: Executive Power Involves the Enforcement of Laws
exact_ocr_heading: 5 EXECUTIVE POWER INVOLVES THE ENFORCEMENT OF LAWS
content_preview: Montesquieu advocated a separate executive for enforcing laws, preferably in a monarch's hands for efficiency, but limited to prevent authoritarianism.

title: The Role of Judiciary is to Interpret the Laws
exact_ocr_heading: 6 THE ROLE OF JUDICIARY IS TO INTERPRET THE LAWS
content_preview: The judiciary interprets laws and ensures fair application, as its proper province. Independence protects citizens from injustice and upholds the rule of law.

title: The Principle of Checks and Balance
exact_ocr_heading: 7 THE PRINCIPLE OF CHECKS AND BALANCE
content_preview: Complete separation is impractical; instead, checks and balances are needed so each organ restrains others, ensuring no branch becomes supreme and preserving political liberty.

title: Importance of Separation of Power
content_preview: Uniting powers leads to tyranny: legislative-executive union results in oppressive rule; legislative-judicial combination erodes justice; executive-judicial fusion enables arbitrary force.

title: Montesquieu as an Aristotle of the 18th Century
exact_ocr_heading: MONTESQUIEU AS AN ARISTOTLE O OF THE 18th CENTURY
content_preview: Montesquieu followed Aristotle's political naturalism, empirical methods, government classification, rule of law promotion, realism over idealism, and comparative-historical approaches, making his wor

title: Conclusion
content_preview: Montesquieu's separation of powers theory is foundational to modern constitutionalism and democratic governance. Influenced by Aristotle's empiricism, he is called the Aristotle of the 18th century.
